latest.title: Director Retirement
latest.content:
17 Jun 2026 10:24
RNS Number : 6664I
DP Aircraft I Limited
17 June 2026
17 June 2026
DP Aircraft I Limited
Director Retirement
DP Aircraft I Limited (the "Group") announces that, following completion of the Group's refinancing and after nearly thirteen years as Chairman, Jon Bridel has decided to retire from the Board. His resignation will take effect on 15 September 2026.
The Board would like to thank Jon for his strong commitment to the Group over many years, including the important role he played to steer the Company through the turbulence of the challenging Covid-19 period. The Board wishes Jon well for the future.
Details of the succession plan will be announced in due course.
Enquiries
For further information, please contact:
Aztec Financial Services (Guernsey) Limited +44(0) 1481 749747
James Lucas

latest.title: Drawdown and Repayment of Debt Facilities
latest.content:
12 Jun 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 0216I
DP Aircraft I Limited
12 June 2026
12 June 2026
DP Aircraft I Limited
Drawdown and Repayment of Debt Facilities
The Board of DP Aircraft I Limited (the "Group") is pleased to announce that the Group has drawn down new debt facilities with a group of lenders led by Investec Bank plc ("Investec") in respect of its two Boeing 787-8 aircraft.
The new financing arrangements provides limited recourse facilities of up to US$92 million. Accordingly, the Board has approved a drawdown of US$86 million, and a further US$6 million remains available for drawdown, subject to the agreed terms and conditions.
The facilities have been utilised principally to refinance the Group's existing debt financing in respect of the aircraft, with additional amounts available to repay certain intergroup indebtedness, associated costs, shareholder loans and for general corporate purposes.
The new facilities mature in late 2034 aligning the debt maturity profile with the end of the first lease period under the Group's lease agreements with LOT Polish Airlines. The loans will amortise over their term to a final balloon repayment at maturity, which may be reduced at the option of the relevant borrowing entity. The facilities include hedging arrangements in respect of the underlying floating rate exposure. Investec will also act as agent, security trustee and hedge counterparty. The all-in financing cost, including hedging costs, has been fixed at approximately 6.9% per annum until the facilities mature in 2034.
Following expiry of the Thai leases in October and December 2026, the aircraft are expected to transfer to the Group's Irish subsidiaries and be delivered to LOT under the previously announced 12-year lease agreements. In accordance with the terms of the new financing arrangements, the facilities have been drawn down by the Guernsey subsidiaries. Following the transfer of the aircraft to the Group's Irish subsidiaries upon commencement of the LOT leases, the related borrower positions will migrate to those Irish subsidiaries.
The Board strongly believes that drawdown of these facilities has materially strengthened the Group's capital structure by refinancing its existing indebtedness ahead of maturity and aligning financing with the long-term LOT lease profile. Accordingly, following the successful drawdown of these facilities, the Board will consider the options available to the Group with the objective of maximising returns for shareholders.
Enquiries
For further information, please contact:
Aztec Financial Services (Guernsey) Limited +44(0) 1481 749747
James Lucas
Investec Bank plc +44(0) 20 7597 4000
David Yovichic / Denis Flanagan
Barclays Bank PLC +44(0) 20 7623 2323
Dion Di Miceli / James Atkinson

latest.title: Refinancing of Existing Debt Facilities
latest.content:
8 May 2026 14:15
RNS Number : 6554D
DP Aircraft I Limited
08 May 2026
8 May 2026
DP Aircraft I Limited
Refinancing of Existing Debt Facilities
The Board of DP Aircraft I Limited (the "Group") is pleased to announce that it has agreed new debt financing with a group of lenders led by Investec Bank plc ("Investec") in respect of its two Boeing 787-8 aircraft.
Under the new arrangements, separate limited recourse facilities of up to US$46 million per aircraft have been made available for MSN 35320 and MSN 36110. The facilities are expected to be drawn initially during May or June 2026 and will be used principally to refinance the Group's existing debt financing in respect of the aircraft, with additional amounts available to repay certain intergroup indebtedness and associated costs and for general corporate purposes. The facilities also provide for an optional second drawdown, subject to the agreed terms and conditions.
The new facilities mature in December 2034 in respect of MSN 35320 and October 2034 in respect of MSN 36110, aligning the debt maturity profile with the end of the first rent period under the Group's lease agreements with LOT Polish Airlines. The loans will amortise over their term to a final balloon repayment at maturity, which may be reduced at the option of the relevant borrowing entity. The facilities include hedging arrangements in respect of the underlying floating rate exposure. Investec will also act as agent, security trustee and hedge counterparty.
The initial drawdowns will refinance the Group's existing aircraft financings, which were otherwise due to mature in October 2026 and December 2026 broadly alongside expiry of the current Thai Airways leases. As previously announced, following expiry of those leases the aircraft are expected to transfer to the Group's Irish subsidiaries and be delivered to LOT under the previously announced 12-year lease arrangements. Under the new financing structure, the facilities will initially be made available to DP Aircraft Guernsey III Limited in respect of MSN 35320 and DP Aircraft Guernsey IV Limited in respect of MSN 36110 and, following the transfer of the aircraft to the Group's Irish subsidiaries in connection with commencement of the LOT leases, the related borrowing positions are expected to transfer to those Irish subsidiaries.
The Board believes that the new facilities materially strengthen the Group's capital structure by refinancing its existing indebtedness ahead of maturity and aligning financing with the long-term LOT lease profile. Further details of the financing costs will be announced following drawdown, once the applicable hedging arrangements have been fixed.
Enquiries
For further information, please contact:
Aztec Financial Services (Guernsey) Limited +44(0) 1481 748833
Chris Copperwaite
Investec Bank plc +44(0) 20 7597 4000
David Yovichic/Denis Flanagan
Barclays Bank PLC +44(0) 20 7623 2323
Dion Di Miceli / James Atkinson

company_30s.what_they_do: DP Aircraft I Limited designs, builds and sells light aircraft and related components for private owners and small commercial operators.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The global light‑jet market is growing as more businesses and wealthy individuals look for flexible, low‑operating‑cost aircraft. DP Aircraft supplies a niche product line that competes with a handful of European and US manufacturers, giving it a defensible position if it can deliver reliable planes and service contracts.
company_30s.current_state: The firm has been under severe financial strain, carrying significant debt and operating at a loss. Management changes and a new loan facility suggest it is trying to stay afloat, but it remains a high‑risk, cash‑burning operation.

rns_narrative: The company has been busy on the governance front: a director retired, a new director was appointed, and a joint corporate broker was named. These moves point to a re‑organisation aimed at stabilising the board.

Financially, DP Aircraft is trimming debt – a repayment of part of its debt facilities was announced, and a new loan facility was secured to cover short‑term needs. The annual report and accounts show a continuing loss, but the debt haircut may give the firm breathing room.
The latest AGM results are still pending, but the company has promised to disclose its cash position and any new contracts that could change its outlook.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet uncertainty – investors are watching but not acting.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A few traders believe the debt restructuring and new loan will give the company enough liquidity to survive and eventually win back market share.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Others fear that the company has no profitable contracts and that the debt relief is only temporary. They worry the firm could be delisted or forced into liquidation.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The conversation has been flat; no recent posts have shifted the tone. If a positive AGM or a new contract announcement arrives, sentiment could swing sharply.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for the ticker is dead – no public search activity, which means a surprise catalyst could create a big spike.

near_term_catalysts: The AGM results (expected in late November) could reveal whether the company has secured new orders or improved its cash position.
The finalisation of the new loan facility, scheduled for the first quarter of 2027, would provide fresh capital and could lift investor confidence.
Any announcement of a new aircraft contract or a partnership with a larger manufacturer would be a strong buying signal.
A sudden spike in trading volume above 10‑day average, coupled with a break above the 52‑week low, would confirm a momentum shift.
expect_next.watch_for: Monitor the stock for a clear breakout past the 52‑week low with at least 1.5× the 10‑day average volume. Also keep an eye on the AGM report for any mention of new revenue streams or a positive cash forecast.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock appears to be in the late stage of its crash cycle – capitulation is largely finished and the price is compressed. A sharp reversal is possible once the debt situation stabilises and the market sees a credible path to profitability.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The price is likely 70‑95 % below its peak, which historically delivers the highest upside when a firm survives. If the company manages to keep its debt under control and secure at least one new contract, a rebound to halfway the pre‑crash level could generate 200‑300 % upside. The signal colour hierarchy (though not explicitly labelled) suggests a deep crash, which is the most rewarding scenario for a contrarian.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The firm is still loss‑making and has a very thin cash buffer. If the AGM shows no new contracts or the loan facility falls through, the company could be forced into liquidation. In that case the price could collapse to a few pence or vanish entirely.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Risk: 100 % of the current price – if the stock falls to zero you lose everything. Reward: 200‑300 % upside if the price recovers to half the pre‑crash level. The asymmetry is clear – the potential gain far outweighs the total loss if the company survives.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter on a breakout above the 52‑week low with volume at least 1.5× the 10‑day average. Size the position so that a 10‑% stop loss would keep losses within your risk tolerance. Hold through the AGM; if the results are positive, consider adding to the position.
playbook.if_watching: Wait for the AGM report or the loan facility announcement. If either confirms new revenue or a stable cash flow, consider a small entry at the 52‑week low breakout. If nothing positive emerges by early Q1 2027, reassess the risk.
playbook.if_passing: If the company declares bankruptcy, is delisted, or fails to secure the new loan, exit immediately and cut losses.

ai_insights.bottom_line: DP Aircraft sits on a deep crash, faces a critical debt‑restructuring deadline, and has a silent market – a classic contrarian setup that could deliver 200‑300 % upside if the company survives its liquidity crunch.
